buckets.payg: Personal/PAYG individual CONTEXT for spend (money out). NOT a deductibility claim — deductibility is decided later at year-end review.
buckets.company: Pty Ltd business expense (money out) — the company books of record.
buckets.property_rented: Expense (money out) on a currently-rented investment property.
buckets.property_vacant: Holding cost (money out) on a vacant/not-genuinely-available property.
buckets.income_business: Money IN (a credit) that is business revenue received by the Pty Ltd — sales, fees, invoices paid.
buckets.income_property: Money IN (a credit) that is rent received on an investment property (pair with the property).
buckets.income_personal: Money IN (a credit) that is personal income — salary/PAYG, interest, dividends received personally.
buckets.refund: Money IN (a credit) that REVERSES a prior expense (a refund/reversal) — nets against spend, it is NOT income.
buckets.asset: Money OUT (a debit) for a CAPITAL purchase — equipment/plant or a capital improvement that DEPRECIATES over its life. NOT an immediate deduction; it becomes a depreciating asset and is flagged for review. Use for clearly capital items (e.g. a laptop/computer, machinery, furniture, an appliance for a rental).

guidance: Categorise each line into exactly one bucket. A line marked money OUT (a debit / spend) takes an expense bucket (payg, company, property_*), or 'asset' if it is a clearly capital purchase that depreciates (large equipment, computers, machinery, furniture). A line marked money IN (a credit) takes an income bucket (income_business, income_property, income_personal) or 'refund' if it reverses an expense. Prefer 'unknown' over guessing — income and capital misclassification are costly. This is general information, not tax advice; never assert deductibility.

_asset_class_note: GENERAL-INFO effective-life defaults for auto-created depreciating assets. Approximations aligned with common ATO TR 2022/1 lives — a sensible starting point only. Auto-created assets still land needs_review for the user to confirm the class, effective life, method and business-use % with a registered tax agent.

claimability.general_info_note: Repairs that restore a rented property to working condition are generally deductible in the year paid (improvements are not).
A new appliance in a rented property is generally a depreciating asset (Div 40), claimed over its effective life — not immediately.
Holding costs are only deductible while the property is genuinely available for rent; vacant land holding costs are generally not deductible since 1 July 2019.
Car expenses on a novated lease are not deductible to the employee — the employer leases the car. The reportable fringe benefits amount (RFBA) is reportable.
Compulsory uniforms, union/professional fees and CPD self-education are commonly deductible for nurses. The initial cost of getting registered is not deductible.
Home-office running costs can be claimed at the 70c/hr fixed rate for 2024-25 and 2025-26; a work computer over $300 is depreciated rather than claimed immediately.

Rent you pay on your own home is generally a private expense and not deductible. Only a sole trader running a genuine 'place of business' from home can apportion occupancy costs — employees and remote workers generally cannot claim rent (only running costs).
Rent on business or commercial premises you lease is generally deductible. If the premises is partly private, only the business-use portion is claimable.
Commercial premises rent paid by your company is generally a deductible business expense. Apportion out any private use.

Additional running costs of working from home (electricity, internet, phone, stationery) can be claimed using the 70c/hr fixed-rate method or the actual-cost method, for the hours you genuinely work from home. You cannot claim occupancy costs (rent, mortgage interest, rates) unless your home is a genuine place of business, and you must keep a record of your hours.

Self-education and professional-development costs are generally deductible when the study has a sufficient connection to your current income-earning work. Study to get a new job or enter a new field is generally not deductible, and apportionment is needed where there is private use.

claimability.merchant_hint: donation,red cross,rspca,salvation army,unicef,oxfam,smith family,beyond blue,cancer council,world vision,charity,dgr
claimability.general_info_note: Gifts and donations to an organisation endorsed as a deductible gift recipient (DGR) are generally deductible above the minimum-gift threshold. The gift must be genuine with nothing material received in return, and you should keep the receipt. Buying raffle tickets, chocolates or attendance at fundraising events is generally not a deductible gift.

Premiums for income-protection (salary-continuance) insurance are generally deductible where the policy covers loss of income, but only the income-protection portion held outside super. Premiums for life, trauma or total-and-permanent-disability cover, and cover held inside superannuation, are generally not deductible.

The cost of managing your tax affairs is generally deductible — including registered tax agent and accountant fees for preparing and lodging your return, ATO interest charges, and travel to obtain tax advice from a recognised adviser.

Annual union fees and subscriptions to trade, business or professional associations connected to your work are generally deductible. The joining fee or a portion attributable to non-work benefits may need to be excluded.

Teachers can generally claim work-related items they pay for themselves such as classroom supplies, teaching resources and the work-use portion of a phone or home internet. Excursion and camp costs you incur for work, and self-education that maintains or improves your teaching skills, may also be claimable. Everyday clothing is not deductible.

Ongoing renewals such as first-aid certificates and working-with-children checks required for your current teaching role are generally deductible. The cost of your initial teacher registration or qualification is not.

Tradespeople can generally claim protective clothing (steel-capped boots, hi-vis, sun protection used outdoors) and the cost of tools and equipment used for work. Tools above the immediate-deduction threshold are depreciated over their effective life rather than claimed immediately, and any private use is apportioned out.

A tradesperson carrying bulky tools or travelling between work sites may be able to claim work-related car expenses using the cents-per-kilometre or logbook method. Normal travel between home and a regular workplace is private and not claimable; keep a logbook or records to support the work-use percentage.

Healthcare workers can generally claim compulsory or logo'd uniforms and their laundry, AHPRA registration renewals, union or professional fees, and CPD self-education that relates to their current role. Plain everyday clothing and the initial cost of getting registered are not deductible.

Office professionals can generally claim the work-use portion of home-office running costs, phone and internet, professional memberships and work-related subscriptions. Equipment above the immediate-deduction threshold is depreciated rather than claimed immediately, and conventional business attire is not deductible.

Drivers who use their own vehicle for work can generally claim work-related car expenses using the cents-per-kilometre or logbook method (not both). Travel between home and a regular workplace is private. A logbook and odometer records are needed to support the actual-cost method.

Hospitality workers can generally claim compulsory or distinctive uniforms (such as chef's whites and logo'd aprons) and their laundry, plus tools of trade like a chef's knife set used for work. Renewals such as RSA and food-handling certificates needed for your current role may also be claimable; plain black-and-white clothing is not.

Sales professionals can generally claim the work-use portion of phone and car expenses for work travel and client visits, plus work-related subscriptions and memberships. Entertaining clients (meals, drinks, events) is generally not deductible, and home-to-work travel is private.

thresholds_by_fy._note: All dollar thresholds in CENTS. These change yearly and are policy-dependent — the depreciation engine + claimability brain read them per FY, never inline. Confirm against ATO before relying on them.

payg_deductibility._note: DENY-BY-DEFAULT deductibility lists for the 'payg' (individual) bucket, used by src/lib/deductibility.ts to stamp a capture state at ingest. GENERAL INFO ONLY — never tax advice; never asserts a $ figure. Matched (case-insensitive substring) against the line's ato_label + merchant. Precedence: deny → apportion → undetermined. The matcher only DENIES private spend or flags APPORTIONMENT; it never auto-asserts a positive deduction — clearly-deductible payg spend (union fees, tax-affairs, donations) stays 'undetermined', is excluded by deny-by-default, and is surfaced for the user to confirm (so resolved_deductible_cents stays ~$0 until a real review). Company spend (separate taxpayer) and capital 'asset' spend are excluded by BUCKET, not here. Deductible claim guidance for these categories lives in the 'claimability' rules above.

deny.note: Groceries and ordinary food are private/domestic spend — generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).
Private or domestic living costs are generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).

Personal loan/credit-card repayments, fees and interest are private or capital in nature — generally not deductible. Only the principal-free interest on a loan used to earn income is deductible, which is captured separately.
Meals and entertainment are generally not deductible (Division 32 / s8-1(2)(b)), even with a work connection, unless a specific exception applies.
The cost of your ordinary daily meals is a private expense and generally not deductible.
Gym, fitness and general health costs are private and generally not deductible (narrow occupation exceptions exist — confirm with a registered tax agent).
Conventional everyday clothing (including swimwear) is private and not deductible (TR 97/12). Only compulsory/occupation-specific or protective clothing is claimable.
Flowers are a private/personal expense and generally not deductible (s8-1(2)(b)).

apportion.note: Additional running costs of working from home can be claimed at the 70c/hr fixed rate (PCG 2023/1) or the actual-cost method for the hours you genuinely work from home — the fixed rate already bundles energy, internet, phone and stationery, so don't also claim those items separately. Needs your hours before it can be claimed.
Self-education is deductible only where the study has a sufficient connection to your CURRENT income-earning work (TR 2024/3). Study to get a new job is not. Needs apportionment for any private use.

Work-related car/travel may be claimed by the cents-per-kilometre (88c/km, max 5,000km for 2024-25) or logbook method. Travel between home and a regular workplace is private. Needs your work-use portion before it can be claimed.

allow_suggest.note: Union fees and professional-association subscriptions directly related to earning your income are generally deductible (s8-1). Confirm the amount and that it relates to your work, then mark it as a deduction.
The cost of managing your tax affairs (registered tax agent fees) is generally deductible (s25-5). Confirm it relates to your tax affairs, then mark it as a deduction.
Gifts of $2 or more to a deductible gift recipient (DGR) are generally deductible (Div 30). Confirm the organisation is a DGR and you received no material benefit, then mark it as a deduction.

Income-protection insurance premiums (outside super) are generally deductible because the benefit would be assessable income (s8-1). Confirm it's not a life/TPD/trauma policy, then mark it as a deduction.
